items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: What distinguishes a heterogeneous from a homogeneous catalyst?
items.correct: A heterogeneous catalyst is in a different phase from the reactants; a homogeneous catalyst is in the same phase.
items.misconception: Heterogeneous catalysts are solid and homogeneous catalysts are liquid — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Heterogeneous catalysts are consumed; homogeneous catalysts are not.
items.why: Students learn 'solid catalyst' examples (iron in Haber) and over-generalise — the actual distinction is about phase relative to the reactants, not the catalyst's physical state alone.
Correct answer: Cu and Zn. “Fe and C” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: biofuel. “coal” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: CO2 makes up approximately what % of atmosphere?
items.why: The real figure is 0.04. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
